EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado struck the city of Loraine resulting in one injury and damage to several light-weight structures and vehicles.  Damage surveyed by a National Weather Service meteorologist began on the south side of the city near Looby Street. From there the tornado traveled northeast producing a one mile long damage path that cut across the southeast corner of the city to near Interstate 20. Along the path, a roof was partially removed from one home and several vehicles and trailers were damaged or destroyed. About $100,000 worth of musical equipment was destroyed when a cargo trailer was rolled by the tornado. The tornado began to dissipate as it crossed Interstate 20 on the east side of the city. Before doing so, it blew two semi-trucks off of the highway, injuring one driver. Several eye witnesses described seeing the tornado's condensation funnel and debris whirling beneath it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system moved across North Central Texas late Friday night February 13th, and during the day Saturday February 14th, producing measurable snow across all but extreme southeast parts of North Texas. Snow began falling Friday night across the southwest parts of North Texas, then spread northeast across the rest of the region late Friday night and early Saturday morning.  The snow  ended  over southwest parts of the region Saturday morning, and across the northeast early Saturday evening. Heavy snow of 4 to 5.5 inches fell on the 14th, from Montague and Wise counties eastward as far as Lamar and Delta counties, and south into northern Tarrant, northern Dallas, and northern Hunt counties.  1 to 3 inches of snow fell across the rest of North Texas, except for a small strip southeast of a Palestine to Hearne line, where only a trace was reported.

A woman drowned after being swept away as she tried to wade across a rain swollen creek near Bicentennial Park.  The heavy rain that caused the creek to rise had ended two to three hours previous to the drowning.
Several vehicles were damaged in the high school and junior high-elementary parking lots. Some homes were also damaged.
A tornado was observed by several storm chasers and spotters touching down in an open field. No damage was reported.

A downburst destroyed a storage building and partially unroofed a house west of Kaufman.  Along FM 987, five homes were destroyed, trees and power lines were blown down.  In the community of Post Oak Bend a mobile home was heavily damaged, a house was partially unroofed, and damage occurred to barns and  trees.  Minor roof damage occurred to several other structures.  The strong winds caused five wrecks on Interstate 20 at mile marker 504.
A brief tornado touchdown was reported.
Hail lasted 15 minutes and covered the ground in Atlanta.
A large number of trees were blown down between the Lake Bardwell Dam and Alma.
A porch was destroyed by high winds.
A shed was destroyed, and 3 barns were damaged between Olin and Fairy.
Hail covered the ground at Negley.

A mobile home was unroofed southeast of Italy and numerous barns and outbuildings were damaged.  A family was trapped in a collapsed mobile home in Avalon, and another mobile home was blown over.Southwest of Avalon a high tension tower was bent over.

A 3 year old 3,000 sq ft house was destroyed.  A warehouse/office building along Interstate 45 had most of it's roof removed.  Another 18 wheeler on Interstate 45 was blown over.

Showers and thunderstorms were widespread across West Texas on the 12th. This activity provided the region with much needed rainfall. Most of the convection was relatively weak, with only occasional cloud-to-ground lightning strikes. During the early afternoon, however, an isolated severe thunderstorm developed over the Big Bend. An amateur radio operator traveling along State Highway 118 reported golfball size hail that covered the ground west of Terlingua Ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of the Davis Mountains and the Big Bend during the afternoon of the 14th. These storms resulted in hail up to the size of quarters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tracked southeast across northern Presidio County and resulted in large hail. Nickel size hail covered the ground along U.S. Highway 90 nineteen miles northwest of Marfa. Quarter size hail accumulated up to three inches deep over the highway just west of Marfa, and crews were required to clear the hail from the roadway. Dime to nickel size hail was also reported at a U.S. Border Patrol check point along U.S. Highway 67 south of Marfa. No damage reports were received.

Outflow from earlier thunderstorms over southeast Texas interacted with a sea breeze boundary and very moist and unstable atmosphere during the afternoon hours of the 13th.  Strong to severe thunderstorms erupted by late afternoon and early evening from Cotulla to George West to Victoria, persisting for several hours and training over several locations producing flooding rainfall.  The thunderstorms eventually organized into a line and pushed offshore during the early morning hours of the 14th.  Extensive flooding crippled the southern half of Bee county, where radar estimated up to 10 to 12 inches of rainfall may have fallen.  Virtually all roads througout the southern half of the Bee county were impassible. Several residents were reported to have been rescued. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive flash flooding occurred across the southern portion of Bee county, mainly south of U.S. 59.  All primary and secondary roads became impassible.  Roads into Tynan, Skidmore and Papalote were all cutt off due to high water.  Several homes, a church and three school buildings were flooded in Skidmore.  Rescues were required for some residents throughout the southern part of the county.  Schools were closed the next day due to impassible roads.  Radar estimates widespread 5 to 8 inches of rain fell over the southern half of the county, south of Beeville.  A bullseye of 8 to 12 inches was estimated to have fallen between Skidmore, Tynan and Olmos.  A spotter recorded 7.7 inches of rain falling in just 2.5 hours. The second supercell storm struck near 635 pm to the northwest of Del Rio near the Los Campos subdivision.  This is west of US 90 and south of Amistad Dam.  Spotters reported that rain and hail struck first, followed by high winds.  Hail varied in size with some as large as softballs and with winds estimated at over 70 mph.  Damage in this part of Del Rio was the worst in the area, consisting of broken windows, dented home exteriors, torn roofs, damaged fences, large trees limbs blown down, trees knocked over, vehicles dented with broken windshields, and livestock injured and killed.  In spite of the considerable damage, the amount of damage in dollars will still likely be less than the massive hailstorm suffered by Del Rio in April of 2002.

Shortly after the severe winds and hail, the first and most powerful of several tornadoes struck.  It was observed by the Val Verde County Emergency Manager as it touched down near 640 pm south of Amistad Lake along Holdup Pass, approximately one mile south of US 90.  The tornado, rated F1on the Fujita scale, was about 100 yards wide.  It first moved eastward destroying the roof of a home and tossing the debris over one-quarter mile along its path.  Occupants of the home had found shelter in their interior closet and were uninjured in spite of considerable damage to the home and the surrounding area.  After tracking eastward for one mile the tornado turned 45 degrees to the right and tracked along mostly open country for almost 3 more miles.  Along the route it tore down power poles, leveled vegetation, damaged roofs and broke windows.  It then lifted and dissipated

The storm system continued to track to the east-southeast, moving over the city of Del Rio.  The Del Rio Emergency Manager reported being in his vehicle on F Street (US 90) as a small vortex formed just in front of him.  He reported that winds were quite strong, but that little moisture or debris was being blown around.  This would have been a small, developing F0 of minimal strength that he reported dissipated within a minute or so.  Based on the 40 mph movement of the storm, the time would have been around 6:47 pm.

The next tornado report was received from Laughlin Air Force Base, where the 709 pm observation noted a tornado.  This small F0 formed west of the Air Base and track eastward...but dissipated quickly as it approached the Base.  No damage was indicated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of severe thunderstorms, including several embedded supercell storms, developed in the vicinity of the Davis and Glass Mountains during the afternoon of the 12th. This activity moved east into the Lower Trans Pecos region of west Texas through the early evening hours. Large hail, severe winds, and at least one tornado accompanied the convective complex. Radar indications suggested that the strongest storms tracked over remote parts of southern Pecos County and northwest Terrell Counties, where several Tornado Warnings were issued based on Doppler radar signatures of intense mesocyclone circulations. Although one tornado was spotted by a sheriff's deputy dispatched to observe the storms, the most dangerous activity remained over open range land.Severe convective development began to spread north along a dryline during the late afternoon and evening hours. A complex of thunderstorms that included at least three supercells affected parts of the Permian Basin, and produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail was reported in Belding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modified tropical airmass remained over much of west Texas on the 29th. Thunderstorms initially developed in the Trans Pecos region of the state and across the plains of southeastern New Mexico. As this activity spread east over the Permian Basin and the Stockton Plateau, heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding. The most significant flooding occurred in the city of Midland where several vehicles stalled when flood waters rushed down city streets.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local officials reported high water flowing over U.S. Highway 90 west of Sanderson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active and very complicated round of severe thunderstorms affected west Texas during the late afternoon and evening of the 5th. Severe weather development was focused in two areas of the region. Convective initiation occurred first near a sub-synoptic scale outflow boundary that was situated across the southeast Permian Basin. This feature was a remnant from overnight convection to the northeast of the area. Two supercell thunderstorms developed along the boundary over rural areas of southeast Midland and northern Upton Counties. As these storms moved east, storm-scale outflow boundaries were observed in local warning radar base reflectivity and velocity data arcing away from the storms toward the west. Both of these boundaries interacted with the larger scale baroclinic boundary under a field of strongly growing convective towers over southern and central Midland County and northeast Crane County. A series of at least four landspout tornadoes were observed as these interactions occurred.The mesoscale boundary began propagating west-northwest across the basin as the supercell storms provided convective enhancement to the cool (east) side of the boundary. Meanwhile strong to severe thunderstorms began propagating east from the mountainous areas of southeast New Mexico and southwest Texas. The convectively enhanced outflow boundary interacted with the complex of storms over the Upper Trans Pecos region. Large hail and two tornadoes were observed in east-central Reeves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Texas Department of Public Safety reported flooding of Farm to Market Road 1450 just east of Pecos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In summary, a significant round of severe thunderstorms affected parts of west Texas during the afternoon and evening of the 11th. At least two supercell storms produced giant hail and one strong tornado over the region.A supercell thunderstorm tracked across Terrell County in the west Texas Lower Trans Pecos region during the late afternoon hours. Multiple reports of large hail including two different instances of tennis ball sized stones were received as this storm tracked east along U.S. Highway 90 between Sanderson and Dryden.A second area of convection erupted over the eastern Permian Basin by late afternoon and continued into the evening hours. A severe storm associated with this activity produced half-dollar size hail in the Westbrook community. An isolated classic supercell evolved from this complex of storms and took on a distinctly deviant southeastward storm motion. This storm took on radar characteristics consistent with a classic tornadic supercell. Hail up to the size of golfballs was observed southeast of Colorado City as the storm evolved into its tornadic phase. A long-lived significant tornado tracked across mainly rural areas of Mitchell County just after sunset. The tornado resulted in three injuries when it crossed State Route 208 and blew a vehicle off of the highway. Severe rear flank downdraft winds also resulted in widespread wind damage south of the tornado's path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second severe thunderstorm developed near Westbrook and resulted in numerous reports of large hail up to the size of golfballs along a broad swath from the city of Westbrook to along State Route 208 south of Colorado City. This storm evolved into a right moving classic supercell as it tracked southeast across the central parts of Mitchell County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Odessa Police Department reported a tornado (landspout) in northeast Crane County near U.S. Highway 385.A series of at least four landspout tornadoes developed as outflow from two isolated severe thunderstorms interacted with a pre-existing boundary remnant from the previous night's convection. Strong towering cumulus clouds above the boundary interactions provided sufficient vertical stretching to result in narrow dust filled vortices that visually extended toward the cloud bases. These landspouts were observed by many people in Midland and law officials in Odessa resulting in numerous reports from both public and local emergency officials.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of severe thunderstorms, including several embedded supercell storms, developed in the vicinity of the Davis and Glass Mountains during the afternoon of the 12th. This activity moved east into the Lower Trans Pecos region of west Texas through the early evening hours. Large hail, severe winds, and at least one tornado accompanied the convective complex. Radar indications suggested that the strongest storms tracked over remote parts of southern Pecos County and northwest Terrell Counties, where several Tornado Warnings were issued based on Doppler radar signatures of intense mesocyclone circulations. Although one tornado was spotted by a sheriff's deputy dispatched to observe the storms, the most dangerous activity remained over open range land.Severe convective development began to spread north along a dryline during the late afternoon and evening hours. A complex of thunderstorms that included at least three supercells affected parts of the Permian Basin, and produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging in size from dimes to golfballs was reported in the city of Alpine. No damage was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Pecos County Sheriff's deputy observed a tornado just east of U.S. Highway 385. The deputy described the tornado as a well-developed cone-shaped vortex, approximately 150 yards in width, that remained in open range land for about two minutes as it moved east. The deputy searched the area for damage after the tornado dissipated, and reported "shredded" mesquite trees in the tornado's path. Otherwise, no damage to man-made structures occurred.
Flooding reported and many roads under water and impassable.
Flooding countywide. Numerous roads closed. FM 1280 in Friday and portions of FM 2781 at Holly Road washed out. FM 358 in Pennington closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In summary, a significant round of severe thunderstorms affected parts of west Texas during the afternoon and evening of the 11th. At least two supercell storms produced giant hail and one strong tornado over the region.A supercell thunderstorm tracked across Terrell County in the west Texas Lower Trans Pecos region during the late afternoon hours. Multiple reports of large hail including two different instances of tennis ball sized stones were received as this storm tracked east along U.S. Highway 90 between Sanderson and Dryden.A second area of convection erupted over the eastern Permian Basin by late afternoon and continued into the evening hours. A severe storm associated with this activity produced half-dollar size hail in the Westbrook community. An isolated classic supercell evolved from this complex of storms and took on a distinctly deviant southeastward storm motion. This storm took on radar characteristics consistent with a classic tornadic supercell. Hail up to the size of golfballs was observed southeast of Colorado City as the storm evolved into its tornadic phase. A long-lived significant tornado tracked across mainly rural areas of Mitchell County just after sunset. The tornado resulted in three injuries when it crossed State Route 208 and blew a vehicle off of the highway. Severe rear flank downdraft winds also resulted in widespread wind damage south of the tornado's path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service damage survey team concluded that a significant tornado struck rural areas of southeastern Mitchell County during the evening of the 11th. The team examined a damage path marked by shredded vegetation (mostly mesquite trees),  downed utility poles, agricultural fences, and a complex of destroyed mobile homes that extended over ten miles in a continuous horseshoe shaped path that crossed State Route 208 twenty miles south-southeast of Colorado City.The tornado developed seventeen miles south-southeast of Colorado City around 2028 CST. Damage indicates the tornado's motion was initially toward the southeast then east as it crossed the heavily traveled State Route 208. Two Texas Tech University students traveling north on 208 (south of the tornado's path) watched as a Ford Expedition drove into the tornadic circulation. The large sports utility vehicle was blown 100 yards off of the highway. Evidence supports the vehicle was rolled a considerable distance by the tornadic winds, but it is unclear whether it became airborne. Three motorists were transferred to local hospitals. One person sustained serious injuries that included a broken back.A detailed damage path analysis and corresponding radar data suggest that the tornado then tracked very slowly east a few miles before curving to the northeast. Similar curved damage paths (turning left of the tornado's original motion) are commonly observed when well-developed tornadoes enter the dissipation stage. The tornado proved to remain very dangerous, however, as eight mobile homes were destroyed by the tornado near the end of its life cycle twenty miles southeast of Colorado City. The light weight and unanchored structures were blown over and shredded by the dissipation stage tornadic winds. The trailers were arranged in a complex and were used to provide shelter for groups of hunters that frequent the ranch property. They were not occupied when the tornado struck.
Power lines and trees blown down.
Power lines and trees down in Throckmorton.
Trees blown down in Woodson.
Emergency manager reported a car blown off the road and many trees downed around the Lohn area.

In addition, an area of very heavy rainfall over the southern Permian Basin resulted in flash flooding across southern Upton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds combined with small hail to wipe out two cotton crops in eastern Gaines County. The winds were strong enough to blow several truck tires a distance of one hundred yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed near the higher terrain of west Texas during the afternoon and evening of the 17th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 100 year-old bank building in Toyah was destroyed when strong winds affected the community on the evening of the 17th. Radar reflectivity data depicted strong to severe thunderstorms well to the west of Toyah in adjacent Culberson County, however, only light reflectivity returns were indicated in the Toyah area and appeared to be associated with anvil blow-off down wind of the severe storms. The Reeves County Emergency Manager reported winds around 50 MPH when the building was destroyed. These winds were likely associated with outflow from the distant storms. The structural integrity of the historic building may have been compromised by the extensive flooding event which affected the community in early April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed over Ward County, and propagated northeast along and just north of Interstate 20. This storm produced golfball size hail near Monahans. Hailstones the size of tennis balls reportedly fell in Gardendale, but reports of damage were minimal. The storm continued to produce large hail to the size of nickels as it moved across northwest Midland County.

Heavy rains on the morning and early afternoon of the 9th produced rainfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour for several hours. Total rainfall amounts of upwards of 10 inches were reported over a 6 mile wide area from southwest San Saba county across the city of San Saba, then on into the northeastern portions of the county. The city of San Saba received the most damage with homes and businesses becoming inundated with water. Also several streets were scoured due to the fast movement of the water. The heavy rains also caused minor flooding on the San Saba river at San Saba and the Colorado river near San Saba.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The large outflow boundary that was enhanced by severe storms over southern Midland and northern Upton Counties continued west across the western Permian Basin and the Upper Trans Pecos. This feature was observed using local warning radar until it interacted with strong to severe convection moving off the higher terrain of southwest Texas. As the outflow boundary encountered this convection near Pecos, local officials observed the first of two tornadoes that occurred in eastern Reeves County south of Pecos near State Highway 17. This tornado may have been a landspout given that at the time the parent thunderstorm was not characterized by severe indicators in reflectivity and velocity Doppler radar data, however, explosive development was noted within the next ten to fifteen minutes prior to the following events. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the central and eastern Permian Basin of west Texas as a cold front began to work its way south into the area. A few of these storms became severe and produced large hail and flash flooding. The most significant activity occurred in the Big Spring area, where hail up to the size of golfballs and extensive flash flooding was reported. Despite the closing of several highways due to high water and reports of high water rescues, no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In summary, a significant round of severe thunderstorms affected parts of west Texas during the afternoon and evening of the 11th. At least two supercell storms produced giant hail and one strong tornado over the region.A supercell thunderstorm tracked across Terrell County in the west Texas Lower Trans Pecos region during the late afternoon hours. Multiple reports of large hail including two different instances of tennis ball sized stones were received as this storm tracked east along U.S. Highway 90 between Sanderson and Dryden.A second area of convection erupted over the eastern Permian Basin by late afternoon and continued into the evening hours. A severe storm associated with this activity produced half-dollar size hail in the Westbrook community. An isolated classic supercell evolved from this complex of storms and took on a distinctly deviant southeastward storm motion. This storm took on radar characteristics consistent with a classic tornadic supercell. Hail up to the size of golfballs was observed southeast of Colorado City as the storm evolved into its tornadic phase. A long-lived significant tornado tracked across mainly rural areas of Mitchell County just after sunset. The tornado resulted in three injuries when it crossed State Route 208 and blew a vehicle off of the highway. Severe rear flank downdraft winds also resulted in widespread wind damage south of the tornado's path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tennis ball size hail was reported at the Terrell County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active and very complicated round of severe thunderstorms affected west Texas during the late afternoon and evening of the 5th. Severe weather development was focused in two areas of the region. Convective initiation occurred first near a sub-synoptic scale outflow boundary that was situated across the southeast Permian Basin. This feature was a remnant from overnight convection to the northeast of the area. Two supercell thunderstorms developed along the boundary over rural areas of southeast Midland and northern Upton Counties. As these storms moved east, storm-scale outflow boundaries were observed in local warning radar base reflectivity and velocity data arcing away from the storms toward the west. Both of these boundaries interacted with the larger scale baroclinic boundary under a field of strongly growing convective towers over southern and central Midland County and northeast Crane County. A series of at least four landspout tornadoes were observed as these interactions occurred.The mesoscale boundary began propagating west-northwest across the basin as the supercell storms provided convective enhancement to the cool (east) side of the boundary. Meanwhile strong to severe thunderstorms began propagating east from the mountainous areas of southeast New Mexico and southwest Texas. The convectively enhanced outflow boundary interacted with the complex of storms over the Upper Trans Pecos region. Large hail and two tornadoes were observed in east-central Reeves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding resulted in running water flowing over State Highway 302 west of Kermit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second supercell storm developed over southeast Andrews County. This storm moved northeast across rural portions of Martin County and across much of southern and eastern Dawson County. The storm produced hail up to the size of golfballs. Due to the rural nature of western Martin County, no reports were received as the storm tracked across the county, however, storm chasers did observe funnel clouds as the storm approached the intersection of State Highways 349 and 176 in west-central Martin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging from the size of quarters to golfballs fell along a swath from the community of Patricia northeast to rural areas of eastern Dawson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third supercell thunderstorm to affect parts of the Permian Basin late on the 12th evolved as part of a large convective complex that developed over the northeastern parts of the basin and the western low rolling plains of west Texas. This storm initiated near Lake J.B. Thomas in southeastern Borden County and moved northeast into Scurry County. This storm exhibited evidence of a persistently strong updraft through its life cycle, but was in the early stages of dissipation when it collapsed and produced a downburst near the city of Snyder.  The mesonet site three miles east of  Snyder measured a wind gust to 58 mph.  Numerous public reports indicated dime to quarter size hail accompanied the thunderstorm along with extreme winds and torrential rains.  Severe winds (likely higher than those measured by the mesonet instruments) resulted in  five uprooted trees with trunks up to eighteen inches in diameter at the Snyder cemetery.  A barn ten miles south of Snyder was also destroyed.  Preliminary damage estimates from hail and wind in and around Snyder were approximately $150,000. In summary, a very active severe weather episode impacted parts of west Texas on the 12th. Supercell thunderstorms initially developed along the higher terrain of the Davis and Glass Mountains. These storms moved east and became potentially tornadic across parts of southern Pecos, northern Brewster, and Terrell Counties.  Damage from these potentially dangerous storms was precluded given their remote locations over rural areas of the west Texas Stockton Plateau and Lower Trans Pecos. Additional severe thunderstorm development occurred late in the afternoon and into the early evening over the Permian Basin. At least three supercell thunderstorms tracked across the basin and resulted in large hail and damaging winds. The most significant damage inflicted by the severe weather event on the 12th occurred in the Snyder vicinity, where an intense downburst was accompanied by hail and damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of severe thunderstorms, including several embedded supercell storms, developed in the vicinity of the Davis and Glass Mountains during the afternoon of the 12th. This activity moved east into the Lower Trans Pecos region of west Texas through the early evening hours. Large hail, severe winds, and at least one tornado accompanied the convective complex. Radar indications suggested that the strongest storms tracked over remote parts of southern Pecos County and northwest Terrell Counties, where several Tornado Warnings were issued based on Doppler radar signatures of intense mesocyclone circulations. Although one tornado was spotted by a sheriff's deputy dispatched to observe the storms, the most dangerous activity remained over open range land.Severe convective development began to spread north along a dryline during the late afternoon and evening hours. A complex of thunderstorms that included at least three supercells affected parts of the Permian Basin, and produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter reported nickel to quarter size hail on the Terrell and Pecos County line along U.S. Highway 90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of slow moving upper level disturbances produced unusually heavy rain across most of North Central Texas in June.  The monthly rainfall ranged from a low of4.08 inches in Eastland County to 20.02 inches at Hudson Oaks in Parker County.  Many locations had twice their normal rainfall for the month.  The DFW Airport had the second wettest June on Record, and had measurable rain 18 days, the most days in any month with measurable rain.  These heavy rains produced periodic flash flooding somewhere in North Central Texas nearly every day of the month.  The month began with a northwest flow event on the 1st, with a large hp supercell producing large hail from Jack County southeast to Parker County.  The storm had an erratic southeast movement, but when the storm entered Northern Parker County, it made a left turn and moved nearly due east through Tarrant County, producing countywide wind damage, with measured winds up to 86 miles an hour at the Meacham Airport, some large hail, and flash flood producing rains.   This became the most destructive storm in Tarrant County since the Mayfest Storm on May 5, 1995.  TXU Electric had their worst power outages in the Companies history, as over 500,000 people were without electricity, some for as long as 5 days.  They had to bring in work crews from out of state to help restore power.    Damage estimates for Tarrant County were well over $100,000,000 from this storm.  Sporadic severe weather occurred the rest of the month, but heavy rain and flooding were the main problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down at Seven Points.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful and nearly-stationary supercell thunderstorm pounded the Falfurrias area with large hail, gusty winds and heavy rain for a period of approximately three hours.  The hail damaged homes, businesses, and automobiles.  A damage survey estimated that approximately 980 homes and businesses suffered damage as a result of the hail, wind and rain.  Also, the Texas Department of Public Safety reported numerous vehicles damaged by the hail.  The supercell thunderstorm was finally forced east of the area as a eastward-moving area of thunderstorms approached the area from the west.

An isolated classic supercell evolved from this complex of storms and took on a distinctly deviant southeastward storm motion. This storm took on radar characteristics consistent with a classic tornadic supercell. Hail up to the size of golfballs was observed southeast of Colorado City as the storm evolved into its tornadic phase. A long-lived significant tornado tracked across mainly rural areas of Mitchell County just after sunset. The tornado resulted in three injuries when it crossed State Route 208 and blew a vehicle off of the highway. Severe rear flank downdraft winds also resulted in widespread wind damage south of the tornado's path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm tracked southeast along U.S. Highway 90 in Terrell County. Multiple reports of large hail ranging in size from quarters to tennis balls were received between Sanderson and the Terrell County Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Terrell County Judge reported tennis ball size hail along U.S. Highway 90 just southeast of Sanderson.
The Austin media reported golfball-sized hail near the intersection of Ben White and IH-35.
A cooperative observer reported trees blown down with lots of minor wind damage in the Hallettsville area.

Shortly afterward, the Val Verde County Sheriff saw a second small F0 tornado touch down just east of Sycamore Creek.  It was also short-lived.  The time of occurrence for it would have been around 731 pm.  Again, no damage was indicated.
Hail fell over much of the eastern half of Comal County, causing damage to some windows of homes and vehicles

Eight people were riding tubes down the Guadalupe River when large hail began falling.  They exited the river and were trying to avoid the hail by hiding under a tree when lightning struck.  The most seriously injured of the group was leaning against the tree trunk.  He suffered a heart stoppage but was resuscitated.  Another victim was reported to have suffered a spinal injury.  All later recovered.
An NWS survey team noted several trees blown down, with numerous large limbs knocked out of the trees.  The event was caused by a small-scale downburst in the Freiheit area.
A Skywarn spotter reported 1 inch hail near the intersection of Ben White and Lamar.
Hail was reported by a Skywarn spotter near the intersection of Stassney and William Cannon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trailer homes damaged by high winds along Hwy 67 in the community of Liberty Eylau.
Trees downed in the town of Redwater.
Trees and power lines downed.

Thunderstorms produced general 1 to 2 inch rainfall over Williamson County with widespread 5 to 6 inch amounts in the eastern half of the county.  Maximum totals were between 8 and 10 inches in the Granger area.  Most rural  roads were closed around Jonah, Granger, Bartlett, Taylor and Thrall due to flash flooding.  Highways SH95 and SH29 were also closed due to high water in the Taylor area.  Around 15 people from the Granger area were evacuated from their homes due to high water and forced to remain in a shelter overnight.  Flooding was also reported at the Granger school.

Streets were flooded in both Kenedy and Karnes City after showers and thunderstorms left rain accumulations of between 1 and 2 inches county-wide, and totals to 5 inches in the south part of the county.  Most roads in the southern part of the county were closed at one time or another due to flash flooding.
Thunderstorms produced general 1 to 2 inch rainfall over Caldwell County, with up to 3 inches west of Lockhart.  Several roads between Martindale and Lockhart were closed due to flash flooding.
Between one-half inch and one inch of rain fell across Wilson County.  Isolated totals in the county were near 3 inches.  Flash flooding began just before midnight and ended shortly after midnight.

An Amateur Radio Spotter in McMahan reported trees knocked over and power lines blown down by high winds.  He measured wind gusts near 48 knots, but estimated they were as high  as 60 knots west of McMahan.  Numerous six-inch limbs were broken off trees and shingles were torn from roofs between Lockhart and McMahan.

The first supercell began to produce severe weather near 6 pm in Comstock. The combination of large hail and damaging winds broke windows in nearly every home and vehicle in Comstock.  In addition, the hail caused widespread minor roof damage.  One house had major roof damage. As this supercell thunderstorm began to move to the east-northeast...a second supercell thunderstorm formed to its southwest and began to move toward the city of Del Rio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow from earlier thunderstorms over southeast Texas interacted with a sea breeze boundary and very moist and unstable atmosphere during the afternoon hours of the 13th.  Strong to severe thunderstorms erupted by late afternoon and early evening from Cotulla to George West to Victoria, persisting for several hours and training over several locations producing flooding rainfall.  The thunderstorms eventually organized into a line and pushed offshore during the early morning hours of the 14th.  Extensive flooding crippled the southern half of Bee county, where radar estimated up to 10 to 12 inches of rainfall may have fallen.  Virtually all roads througout the southern half of the Bee county were impassible. Several residents were reported to have been rescued. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms poured a general 2 in rain over Wilson county, with widespread 4 inch amounts in the south part of the county, and a maximum of 11 inches at Poth  Flooding and flash flooding was widespread in the Richland Heritage Subdivision in Poth.  Minor damage was indicated to dozens of homes.  One woman drove her vehicle into deep water but was able to exit the vehicle with her two children and get to safety.  The vehicle was found by rescue workers to be completely submerged.  Most roads in southern Wilson County were closed during the late afternoon due to flash flooding, including SH181 near Poth.

Streets and rural roads were covered by flash flooding as general 1 inch rains fell over Bastrop County.  Up to 4 inches was indicated in the south part of the county.  The flash flooding  was short-lived.
Rainfall averaging almost 2 inches over Caldwell county, with up to 4 over the north part of the county.  The rain produced flash flooding that closed many of the county's roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level disturbance moved out of Northern Mexico and into to South Texas during the morning of the 6th.  Low level winds ahead of the system backed to the east as winds increased in the mid levels resulting in strong deep layer shear.  Several supercell thunderstorms developed inland over the Coastal Bend.  An HP supercell moved from near Beeville into south-central Goliad county and through northern Refugio, southern Victoria and Calhoun counties.  NWS radar estimated a wide swath of 2-3 inches of rainfall across this area, with localized amounts of 3 to 4 inches, producing flash flooding.  In addition, this storm produced three F0 tornadoes along it's path through Southern Victoria and Calhoun counties.  Additional rotating storms across Kleberg and Nueces county produced flash flooding in Nueces county as well as one confirmed F0 tornado along the coast in Northeast Kleberg county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow from earlier thunderstorms over southeast Texas interacted with a sea breeze boundary and very moist and unstable atmosphere during the afternoon hours of the 13th.  Strong to severe thunderstorms erupted by late afternoon and early evening from Cotulla to George West to Victoria, persisting for several hours and training over several locations producing flooding rainfall.  The thunderstorms eventually organized into a line and pushed offshore during the early morning hours of the 14th.  Extensive flooding crippled the southern half of Bee county, where radar estimated up to 10 to 12 inches of rainfall may have fallen.  Virtually all roads througout the southern half of the Bee county were impassible. Several residents were reported to have been rescued. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms affected the northeast Permian Basin of west Texas during the late afternoon and evening of the 25th. These storms developed near a dryline/cold front triple point. A highly visible tornado was observed by many residents of Snyder in Scurry County. The parent thunderstorm produced large hail it tracked northeast over the city. A second supercell tracked east, then southeast across northern Mitchell County. This storm produced two tornadoes, baseball size hail, and damaging winds in the vicinity of Colorado City and Loraine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of hail ranging from pea size to quarter size were received from the Snyder vicinity as a supercell thunderstorm tracked across the area.

The heaviest rainfall occurred in the northern part of Travis County, where up to 4 inches was indicated between Lago Vista and Pflugerville.  One foot of water was reported over the road at Anderson Lane.  Water was reported to be between 3 and 4 feet deep over the road in Pflugerville, where two vehicles became stranded in the flood.  One of the drivers had become stuck in a low-water crossing on Pfluger Lane just east of FM685, when high water carried the vehicle into a creek.  A woman and her daughter were rescued from their flooded car in another high water situation.  In all,  numerous vehicle water rescues were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough remained situated out to the west in Northern Mexico and the Southwest U.S.  A very unstable airmass developed during the afternoon hours across South Texas triggering thunderstorms.  An upper level shortwave rotated out of Mexico during the afternoon providing increasing large-scale ascent, divergence aloft and steepening mid-level lapse rates.  Thunderstorms initially began as supercells across the Rio Grande Plains and Duval and Jim Wells counties during the afternoon and early evening, producing large hail and heavy rain.  The supercells organized into a squall-line during the evening and moved east across the Coastal Bend, producing heavy rainfall and flash flooding.  A wake low formed over the Coastal Bend behind the squall line as it moved into the offshore waters during the late night and early morning hours.  High sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph, with some gusts over 60 mph, developed over the Coastal Bend, Victoria Crossroads region and the bays and nearshore waters.  Damage associated with these winds was mainly confined to tree limbs, however a tug boat in Port Oconnor was reportedly damaged.  See High Wind event entry as well as wind entries under Gulf of Mexico for further information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sqaull line moved into the Coastal Bend producing heavy rainfall.  A widespread 1 to 3 inches fell over the southern Coastal Bend, including Kleberg county.  Several Farm to Market and County roads were flooded.  Radar estimated 3 to 4 inches of rain fell, where the squall line merged with a supercell.  Highway 285 was reported flooded and closed in southwest Kleberg county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of severe thunderstorms affected the Lower Trans Pecos and the eastern Permian Basin regions of West Texas during the afternoon and evening of the 11th. An isolated supercell thunderstorm produced large hail over the northwest part of Terrell County. Less organized multicell convection resulted in reports of large hail and flash flooding over the eastern Permian Basin during the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to nickel size hail was reported in Colorado City.

Rainfall in the northern half of Williamson County averaged between 2 and 3 inches, with isolated totals to near 4 inches.  SH29 was closed in eastern Williamson County.  SH685 was reported to be closed due to flooding from Brushy Creek.  Lake Creek was also reported out of banks.  One of the greatest concerns from the heavy rain was that it caused more than 100,000 gallons of sewage to escape from the Brush Creek Regional Sewerage System.  It flowed out of a manhole and into Brushy Creek.

Rainfall over Kendall County averaged at least one inch, with two inches falling over the northern part of the county and up to 3 inches in the northwest corner.  Several rural roads in northwest Kendall County were closed due to flash flooding.
General 1 inch rains fell over Burnet County, with totals near 3 inches south of Marble Falls.  Several roads in the southern part of the county were closed due to flash flooding.
Between 2 and 3 inches of rain fells in the Barksdale area, closing SH55 due to flash flooding.  Two persons were rescued from a flooded vehicle just south of Barksdale.
The Frio River at Concan crested at 18.23 feet on April 6 at 1230CST, where flood stage is 11 feet.
The Frio River near Uvalde crested at 16.72 feet on April 6 at 1800CST, where flood stage is 12 feet.

Rainfall in Dimmit County averaged one-half inch with nearly 3 inches west  of Carrizo Springs and up to 6 inches near Big Wells.  Flash flooding closed several roads in Carrizo Springs through the mid afternoon

Tornado damaged shopping center at Ranchester and Bellaire intersection. Shopping center building received significant damage to its eastern rear and western front windows. Cars in shopping center parking lot were damaged with one overturned on its side. A residential neighborhood on the south side of Bellaire Street, Sharpstown County Club Terrace, received tree and fencing damage. Reports of cars overturned and window damage at the intersection of Bellaire Street and Hwy 8 approximately 1/2 mile west of the aforementioned shopping center (Key Map 530E-J).
Penny-size hail reported at Beechnut and Hwy 6 intersection (Key Map 528J).
Trees down and fence damage between Bellaire and Beechnut Streets in SW Bellaire. Gas station canopy blown off at the intersection of Beechnut and I-610 (Key Map 531L-M).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms containing large and damaging hail along with a few tornadoes and flooding rainfall moved across the Texas panhandle during the evening hours. The hardest hit areas from the large hail and tornadoes were from northwest Amarillo to the northern sections of Canyon where the hospital district and Baptist St. Anthony's hospital sustained major damage to windows. A tornado just east of Canyon produced major damage. The total cost of the hail was approximately one hundred and seventy-five million dollars. The Insurance Council of Texas has ranked this event as the sixth costliest hail storm in Texas history. No injuries were reported from the tornadoes and hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of slow moving upper level disturbances produced unusually heavy rain across most of North Central Texas in June.  The monthly rainfall ranged from a low of4.08 inches in Eastland County to 20.02 inches at Hudson Oaks in Parker County.  Many locations had twice their normal rainfall for the month.  The DFW Airport had the second wettest June on Record, and had measurable rain 18 days, the most days in any month with measurable rain.  These heavy rains produced periodic flash flooding somewhere in North Central Texas nearly every day of the month.  The month began with a northwest flow event on the 1st, with a large hp supercell producing large hail from Jack County southeast to Parker County.  The storm had an erratic southeast movement, but when the storm entered Northern Parker County, it made a left turn and moved nearly due east through Tarrant County, producing countywide wind damage, with measured winds up to 86 miles an hour at the Meacham Airport, some large hail, and flash flood producing rains.   This became the most destructive storm in Tarrant County since the Mayfest Storm on May 5, 1995.  TXU Electric had their worst power outages in the Companies history, as over 500,000 people were without electricity, some for as long as 5 days.  They had to bring in work crews from out of state to help restore power.    Damage estimates for Tarrant County were well over $100,000,000 from this storm.  Sporadic severe weather occurred the rest of the month, but heavy rain and flooding were the main problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down in Groesbeck and across the county.
Hail to the size of pennies was reported by the local emergency manager.
Thunderstorms dropped between 1 and 3 inches of rain over western Fayette County between Muldoon and Cistern.  Flash flooding closed FM2237, FM154 and FM1115 through the late afternoon.
Severe thunderstorm winds blew down trees and power lines in and near Moulton and produced nearly 3.5 inches of rain in just one hour.

A general 1 inch rain fell across Lee County.  Maximum totals were near 3 inches in the area between Lincoln, Giddings, and Dime Box.  Flash flooding was widespread along county roads through the evening.
Widespread 1 inch rainfall with as much as 3 inches between Jarrell and Florence and also between Taylor and Copeland, caused flash flooding through the late evening.

Between 2 and 3 inches of rain fell in the mid afternoon between Flatonia and Schulenburg.  Maximum totals were reported to be near 4 inches between the two towns.  Flash flooding closed several roads, including FM209 and SH95.

Between 1 and 2 inches of rain fell across Kendall County in the late afternoon.  Reports indicated that up to 4 inches had fallen in the southeast part of the county.  Flash flooding was reported across most county rural roads.

The month began with a northwest flow event on the 1st, with a large hp supercell producing large hail from Jack County southeast to Parker County.  The storm had an erratic southeast movement, but when the storm entered Northern Parker County, it made a left turn and moved nearly due east through Tarrant County, producing countywide wind damage, with measured winds up to 86 miles an hour at the Meacham Airport, some large hail, and flash flood producing rains.   This became the most destructive storm in Tarrant County since the Mayfest Storm on May 5, 1995.  TXU Electric had their worst power outages in the Companies history, as over 500,000 people were without electricity, some for as long as 5 days.  They had to bring in work crews from out of state to help restore power.    Damage estimates for Tarrant County were well over $100,000,000 from this storm.  Sporadic severe weather occurred the rest of the month, but heavy rain and flooding were the main problems.

Severe thunderstorms moved across the western half of the Texas panhandle producing large hail and damaging winds along with a couple of brief tornadoes. Very heavy rainfall from the thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding over the central Texas panhandle. No injuries were reported from the tornadoes and the large hail...however the high winds did cause some damage.

Flooding across western and northwestern Tarrant County including numerous high water rescues.  Farmers Branch Creek in White Settlement flooded the town, causing major damage to 50 homes and minor damage to 150 homes.

There were numerous reports of flooding  on secondary and county roads.
The Pilot Point ISD had 3 buildings flooded.
Lightning strikes blew two big breakers and damaged two houses.
A metal swing set weighing 3,000 pounds was moved a short distance and damaged.
Every street in town was flooded.
Lightning set two oil storage tanks on fire.

Severe thunderstorms containing large and damaging hail along with a few tornadoes and flooding rainfall moved across the Texas panhandle during the evening hours. The hardest hit areas from the large hail and tornadoes were from northwest Amarillo to the northern sections of Canyon where the hospital district and Baptist St. Anthony's hospital sustained major damage to windows. A tornado just east of Canyon produced major damage. The total cost of the hail was approximately one hundred and seventy-five million dollars. The Insurance Council of Texas has ranked this event as the sixth costliest hail storm in Texas history. No injuries were reported from the tornadoes and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at the Westgate Mall area of west Amarillo. Many roofs and skylights to homes damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms affected the west Texas Permian Basin and Upper Trans Pecos region during the evening of the 6th. The most significant damage from this activity occurred in Winkler County where giant hail damaged vehicles and melon crops near Wink.An organized linear complex of storms tracked southeast across the eastern Permian Basin shortly after midnight LST. Severe thunderstorm winds broke large tree limbs and blew a car off of a local highway as regional radars depicted a bow-shaped echo moving southeast across Dawson, Scurry, and Mitchell Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public and media reports indicated that tennis ball size hail broke windows out of vehicles and caused damage to melon crops near Wink.

Major street flooding occurred across Corpus Christi as a result of slow moving and training thunderstorms.  Several city streets throughout central and south Corpus Christi were flooded.  Radar estimated 2 to 4 inches of rain fell across this area of Corpus Christi.  Spotters reported 4 inches of rain on Ocean Drive and 3 inches of rain in just 1 hours and 15 minutes at Kostoryz and Golihar.
Radar estimated between 2 and 3 inches of rain fell over Kingsville.  6th street in Kingsville was floodeed and impassable.  Several other city streets experienced minor flooding.

Heavy rainfall resulted in flooding on Interstate 35 near mile marker 50 as well as low lying flooding in and around Encinal.  Doppler radar estimated between 2 and 5 inches of rainfall fell across southwestern La Salle county and northern Webb county.  An NWS Cooperative Observer in Encinal recorded 5.20 inches of rainfall.

Heavy rainfall resulted in several low lying roads across northern Calhoun being flooded briefly.  Doppler radar estimated between 2 and 4 inches of rain fell from Port Alto to Point Comfort to Port Lavaca.

Heavy rainfall resulted in flooding on Highway 444 across eastern Victoria County, near Inez.  In addition, several smaller secondary roads near creeks were briefly flooded.  Doppler radar estimated between 1 and 3 inches of rainfall fell across areas of far eastern Victoria County.

Heavy rain fell from late night into the early morning hours over southern Edwards, southern Real, and northern Uvalde Counties.  Totals of 1 to 2 inches were widespread across the three county area, with the heaviest rainfall of up to 6 inches between Barksdale, Leakey, and Concan.  Flash flooding continued through the early morning and past the sunrise hour.

Just as with Uvalde County, early warnings to the camps by the Real County Sheriff prevented any serious threats to life.

The DFW Airport had the second wettest June on Record, and had measurable rain 18 days, the most days in any month with measurable rain.  These heavy rains produced periodic flash flooding somewhere in North Central Texas nearly every day of the month.  The month began with a northwest flow event on the 1st, with a large hp supercell producing large hail from Jack County southeast to Parker County.  The storm had an erratic southeast movement, but when the storm entered Northern Parker County, it made a left turn and moved nearly due east through Tarrant County, producing countywide wind damage, with measured winds up to 86 miles an hour at the Meacham Airport, some large hail, and flash flood producing rains.   This became the most destructive storm in Tarrant County since the Mayfest Storm on May 5, 1995.  TXU Electric had their worst power outages in the Companies history, as over 500,000 people were without electricity, some for as long as 5 days.  They had to bring in work crews from out of state to help restore power.    Damage estimates for Tarrant County were well over $100,000,000 from this storm.  Sporadic severe weather occurred the rest of the month, but heavy rain and flooding were the main problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs wereblown down at the Worth Ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds across the western and central Texas panhandle during the afternoon and evening hours. No injuries were reported from these storms...however some damage did occur with the high winds.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds across the western and central Texas panhandle during the afternoon and evening hours. No injuries were reported from these storms...however some damage did occur with the high winds.

Severe thunderstorms containing large and damaging hail along with a few tornadoes and flooding rainfall moved across the Texas panhandle during the evening hours. The hardest hit areas from the large hail and tornadoes were from northwest Amarillo to the northern sections of Canyon where the hospital district and Baptist St. Anthony's hospital sustained major damage to windows. A tornado just east of Canyon produced major damage. The total cost of the hail was approximately one hundred and seventy-five million dollars. The Insurance Council of Texas has ranked this event as the sixth costliest hail storm in Texas history. No injuries were reported from the tornadoes and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at Tascosa Road and Amarillo Boulevard.

The Ft Worth Zoo was closed due to high water and 250 guests were evacuated. 40 vehicles in the parking lot  had flood damage.  Several businesses were damaged from high water.  A car was swept away at the intersection of Butler and McClure streets.
The Six Flags Over Texas amusement park was flooded by Johnson Creek and closed.  6 persons were rescued from a railroad car where they had taken refuge from high water.
Jacksboro Highway shut down due to high water.  At 1215 PM there was a high water rescue on Kenwood Street.
Water covered the intersection of Highway 53 and FM 485.
Water covered Highway 287 near Reagor Springs.
CR 1114 near FM 4 was flooded.
12 homes were flooded with widespread street flooding.

Showers and thunderstorms dropped widespread 1 to 2 inch amounts from western Bandera and western Medina Counties across northern Uvalde into most of Edwards and Real Counties.  Highest amounts were near 6 inches near Vanderpool.  Flash flooding was indicated through the afternoon.

Showers and thunderstorms dropped widespread 1 to 2 inch amounts from western Bandera and western Medina Counties across northern Uvalde into most of Edwards and Real Counties.  Highest amounts were near 6 inches between Barksdale and Camp Wood.  Flash flooding was indicated through the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds moved across the western...central and southern Texas panhandle during the late afternoon and early evening hours. Damage due the high winds occurred over the western Texas panhandle...however no injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3 and 4 inches of rain fell in the D'Hanis area during the mid afternoon.  Flash flooding along Parker Creek east of D'Hanis and along Seco Creek west of D'Hanis cut US90 in both locations, isolating the town.  Around 30 families were evacuated from the two creeks.  Minor damage was reported to homes in the area.  No injuries were indicated.
Golfball sized hail reported on FM 324.
Power lines downed on FM 2108.
Trees downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In summary, widespread heavy rainfall and flash flooding over west Texas on Sunday, July 25th resulted in nearly $3 million worth of damage. The hardest it area was the west Texas Lower Trans Pecos region in the vicinity of Terrell County. Extensive damage occurred along Texas State Highway 349. Several waves of flash flooding along area rivers and creeks threatened structures and motorists. No injuries or fatalities resulted from event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the mid morning hours Sunday, infrared satellite imagery depicted rapid cooling of cloud top temperatures over the southern Big Bend as thunderstorm activity increased. This activity resulted in flash flooding across portions of the national and state parks. State Route 170 was inundated by high water at several locations when Terlingua Creek and Long Draw flooded near Study Butte.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Park Service and the local media reported that flash flooding along Alamo Creek resulted in flowing water that inundated State Route 170 east of Study Butte. In addition, flash flooding of low lying areas resulted in hazardous driving conditions along State Highway 118 north of the city.

1 to 2 inch rain totals over eastern Bandera County, with up to 3 inches in the Pipe Creek area, produced flash flooding along FM1283, as well as many other east county roads.

Evening and late night showers and thunderstorms produced a general 1 to 2 inch rainfall over Medina County, as well as the western half of Bexar County.  Highest totals were 6 inches between D'Hanis, Helotes and Leon Springs.  Widespread flash flooding was indicated in the US90 corridor between D'Hanis and the western edge of San Antonio.
Trees were downed across town.
Strong wind gusts caused heavy damage at the Wilbarger County Airport. Eleven hangers were damaged as well as nine aircraft. Seven of these aircraft were considered total losses.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: General 1 inch rainfall across southern DeWitt, western Lavaca, and eastern Karnes Counties from Speaks to Hallettsville to Cuero to Nordheim to Helena to Kenedy, with isolated amounts near 2 inches, produced spotty flash flooding through the afternoon.
Showers and thunderstorms produced between 2 and 3 inches of rain in and around the Brackettville area.  Flash flooding was reported along FM334, FM131, FM674 and FM693.
Hail was reported near Anderson Mill Road.
Severe thunderstorm winds blew over trees in the Oak Hill section of Austin.

Heavy rain from northwestern Bexar County into southern Kendall County produced general 1 to 2 inch amounts, with isolated totals to 3 inches.  Many rural roads over the two counties were impassible due to high water through the early evening.

Thunderstorms over northern Atascosa County and southern Bexar County produced general 1 to 2 inch totals, with isolated pockets of 3 inch amounts near Lytle, near Somerset, and between Elmendorf and St. Hedwig.  Brief flash flooding developed over the two counties, with the worst along their common county line.

Thunderstorms over northern Atascosa County and southern Bexar County produced general 1 to 2 inch totals, with isolated pockets of 3 inch amounts near Lytle, near Somerset, and between Elmendorf and St. Hedwig.  Brief flash flooding developed over the two counties, with the worst along their common county line.

Heavy rain began falling shortly after midnight over eastern Comal County, southern Hays, and western Caldwell Counties, as well as most of Guadalupe County.  The four county area received between 1 and 2 inches, with as much as 4 inches falling in parts of San Marcos, and between New Braunfels, Seguin, and Maxwell.  Flash flooding closed numerous roads between the three cities from near sunrise into the mid morning.
Onion Creek at Driftwood crested at 10.56 feet on June 30 at 0740CST, where flood stage is 7 feet.

Local law enforcement reported a tornado just west of Roma.  The tornado moved very slowly south with time and produced no damage before dissipating.

Slow moving and training thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall across Nueces and San Patricio counties.  Widespread flooding of streets in Portland was reported. Radar estimated 2 to 4 inches of rain fell across this area, over already saturated grounds.

Widespread 1 to 2 inch rain totals, with up to 5 inches in the Lytle area, caused flash flooding from late morning through the afternoon and into the evening.

Showers and thunderstorms dropped between 1 and 2 inches of rain over much of Guadalupe County.  Highest totals were near 5 inches from Seguin  southward along SH123 into the southern part of the county.  Flash flooding continued from late morning through the afternoon and into the evening.

Between 1 and 2 inches of rain fell over much of Bexar, Comal, Hays and Kendall Counties in the morning.  Maximum rain amounts were 3 inches around Somerset.  Flash flooding developed in the late morning and continued into the afternoon.

Showers and thunderstorms produced a general 1 to 2 inch rain over Caldwell County, with as much as 5 inches between Lockhart and Luling.  Flash flooding was reported from late morning into the evening hours.

Between 1 and 2 inches of rain fell over much of Bexar, Comal, Hays and Kendall Counties in the morning.  Maximum rain amounts were 3 inches near New Braunfels.  Flash flooding developed in the late morning and continued into the afternoon.
Showers and thunderstorms dropped between 1 and 2 inches of rain over the saturated soils of Comal County south of SH46.  Flash flooding was reported around the noon period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms affected the west Texas Permian Basin and Upper Trans Pecos region during the evening of the 6th. The most significant damage from this activity occurred in Winkler County where giant hail damaged vehicles and melon crops near Wink.An organized linear complex of storms tracked southeast across the eastern Permian Basin shortly after midnight LST. Severe thunderstorm winds broke large tree limbs and blew a car off of a local highway as regional radars depicted a bow-shaped echo moving southeast across Dawson, Scurry, and Mitchell Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were broken by severe thunderstorm winds. A rural resident of eastern Borden County measured a 60 MPH wind gust with a home weather station.
Rainfall between 1 and 2 inches, with totals up to 3 inches began falling near noon over Fayette and Lavaca Counties east of US77.  Flash flooding was reported in the late afternoon period.

Southern Atascosa County received between 1 and 2 inches during the morning period, with just over 3 inches falling in the southwestern corner near Hindes. Several roads remained under water through the morning period.

Between 1 and 2 inches of rain fell across Frio County during the pre-dawn hours.  Up to 3 inches was indicated between Pearsall and Derby.  FM1581 was reported under water in several places due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of slow moving upper level disturbances produced unusually heavy rain across most of North Central Texas in June.  The monthly rainfall ranged from a low of4.08 inches in Eastland County to 20.02 inches at Hudson Oaks in Parker County.  Many locations had twice their normal rainfall for the month.  The DFW Airport had the second wettest June on Record, and had measurable rain 18 days, the most days in any month with measurable rain.  These heavy rains produced periodic flash flooding somewhere in North Central Texas nearly every day of the month.  The month began with a northwest flow event on the 1st, with a large hp supercell producing large hail from Jack County southeast to Parker County.  The storm had an erratic southeast movement, but when the storm entered Northern Parker County, it made a left turn and moved nearly due east through Tarrant County, producing countywide wind damage, with measured winds up to 86 miles an hour at the Meacham Airport, some large hail, and flash flood producing rains.   This became the most destructive storm in Tarrant County since the Mayfest Storm on May 5, 1995.  TXU Electric had their worst power outages in the Companies history, as over 500,000 people were without electricity, some for as long as 5 days.  They had to bring in work crews from out of state to help restore power.    Damage estimates for Tarrant County were well over $100,000,000 from this storm.  Sporadic severe weather occurred the rest of the month, but heavy rain and flooding were the main problems.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds across the western and central Texas panhandle during the afternoon and evening hours. No injuries were reported from these storms...however some damage did occur with the high winds.

Rainfall over the northwest part of Caldwell county averaged between 1 and 2 inches, with the stretch from Lockhart to Mendoza receiving over 3 inches.  Most rural roads in the northwest part of the county were closed due to high water.
Man collapsed and died while mowing lawn.

Public reported nickle sized hail along Highway 239 northwest of Tivoli.

Slow moving and training thunderstorms resulted in flooding across areas of Corpus Christi.  Several city streets, intersections and underpasses were flooded.  The slick roadways contributed to several accidents throughout the city.  A portion of the Crosstown Expressway, from S.P.I.D. to Gollihar, was closed and impassable with up to 2 feet of water covering the roadway.  Radar estimated widespread 2 to 4 inches of rain fell, with isolated areas of 5 to 8 inches, across central sections of Corpus Christi and up into Portland.  6.1 inches of rain was reported by a spotter on Brawner Parkway and 7.6 inches was reported by the public off of Kostoryz and Gollihar.

Strong winds caused by a heatburst were reported in the Kamay, Iowa Park, and Wichita Falls areas. Wind gusts ranged from 60 mph to 72 mph across the area with the highest measured gust of 72 mph reported at the high school stadium on the southwest side of Wichita Falls.
Power lines were downed.
Several 6 to 8 inch diameter tree limbs were downed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storms produced a general 1 inch rainfall over the eastern half of Travis County.  The highest amounts were 3 inches in the Del Valle area.  Many of the rural roads in eastern Travis County were closed shortly after midnight due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms containing large and damaging hail along with a few tornadoes and flooding rainfall moved across the Texas panhandle during the evening hours. The hardest hit areas from the large hail and tornadoes were from northwest Amarillo to the northern sections of Canyon where the hospital district and Baptist St. Anthony's hospital sustained major damage to windows. A tornado just east of Canyon produced major damage. The total cost of the hail was approximately one hundred and seventy-five million dollars. The Insurance Council of Texas has ranked this event as the sixth costliest hail storm in Texas history. No injuries were reported from the tornadoes and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at Interstate 40 and Climer Circle.

Storms over Comal County produced general 1 inch totals, with isolated 3 inch amounts in the area around Spring Branch. Several roads were closed due to high water, especially in the northwest part of the county.
Rainfall averaged nearly 1 inch in Hays County, with totals of up to 3 inches near Buda.  Numerous roads were closed in the east half of the county.

A supercell moved across the town of Freer resulting in damage on west and south side of town.  Freer Police reported large tree limbs down, roof damage to an older home, a patio cover blown off a home, and downed telephone poles, including a new pole snapped in half.  In addition, rain guages recorded close to 4.5 inches of rain.
Radar estimated 2 inches of rain fell quickly over already saturated grounds in nothern Calhoun county.  A portion of HIghway 87 in Port Lavaca was briefly impassable due to high water.
Several reports of funnel clouds off of FM 521.

Numerous reports of nickel to quarter size hail also accompanied the storm from West Odessa to the Gardendale Community in northeast Ector County. Hailstones the size of tennis balls reportedly fell in Gardendale, but reports of damage were minimal. The storm continued to produce large hail to the size of nickels as it moved across northwest Midland County.

Severe thunderstorms containing large and damaging hail along with a few tornadoes and flooding rainfall moved across the Texas panhandle during the evening hours. The hardest hit areas from the large hail and tornadoes were from northwest Amarillo to the northern sections of Canyon where the hospital district and Baptist St. Anthony's hospital sustained major damage to windows. A tornado just east of Canyon produced major damage. The total cost of the hail was approximately one hundred and seventy-five million dollars. The Insurance Council of Texas has ranked this event as the sixth costliest hail storm in Texas history. No injuries were reported from the tornadoes and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at Central Avenue and the Dumas Highway or U.S. Highway 87/287.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of slow moving upper level disturbances produced unusually heavy rain across most of North Central Texas in June.  The monthly rainfall ranged from a low of4.08 inches in Eastland County to 20.02 inches at Hudson Oaks in Parker County.  Many locations had twice their normal rainfall for the month.  The DFW Airport had the second wettest June on Record, and had measurable rain 18 days, the most days in any month with measurable rain.  These heavy rains produced periodic flash flooding somewhere in North Central Texas nearly every day of the month.  The month began with a northwest flow event on the 1st, with a large hp supercell producing large hail from Jack County southeast to Parker County.  The storm had an erratic southeast movement, but when the storm entered Northern Parker County, it made a left turn and moved nearly due east through Tarrant County, producing countywide wind damage, with measured winds up to 86 miles an hour at the Meacham Airport, some large hail, and flash flood producing rains.   This became the most destructive storm in Tarrant County since the Mayfest Storm on May 5, 1995.  TXU Electric had their worst power outages in the Companies history, as over 500,000 people were without electricity, some for as long as 5 days.  They had to bring in work crews from out of state to help restore power.    Damage estimates for Tarrant County were well over $100,000,000 from this storm.  Sporadic severe weather occurred the rest of the month, but heavy rain and flooding were the main problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell near White Rock Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms affected the west Texas Permian Basin and Upper Trans Pecos region during the evening of the 6th. The most significant damage from this activity occurred in Winkler County where giant hail damaged vehicles and melon crops near Wink.An organized linear complex of storms tracked southeast across the eastern Permian Basin shortly after midnight LST. Severe thunderstorm winds broke large tree limbs and blew a car off of a local highway as regional radars depicted a bow-shaped echo moving southeast across Dawson, Scurry, and Mitchell Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local radio broadcaster reported thunderstorm winds as "exceptional" in Colorado City. A vehicle was blown off of the highway just west of the city. No injuries were reported.

The first of two flash floods along the Pecos River in northeastern Terrell County occurred just before sunrise. Runoff from the Dry Creek flood that damaged State Highway 349 emptied into the Pecos River from the lower portion of Independence Creek. The Chandler Ranch headquarters, located in a flood plain adjacent to the merger of Independence Creek and the Pecos River, sustained serious damage. Ranch residents described a large "wall" of water that swept through the flood plain and inundated the headquarters. Large trees were uprooted by the currents. A swimming pool was destroyed by debris carried in the raging water. Several structures, including a permanent residence, were severely damaged. Damage to the headquarters was aggravated by a second significant flood wave that propagated down the Pecos and produced another round of flash flooding on the grounds late Sunday evening.

Although roads were closed due to the high water...no damage or injuries were reported from the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours produced damaging winds and damaging large hail as they moved south across the southern Texas panhandle. The damaging winds blew down power lines just north of the city of Canyon.  Damaging large hail caused major roof damage to homes from the western sections of Amarillo south to just north and into the northwest and northern sections of Canyon.  Home windows and car windshields were also broken from the large hail.  No injuries were reported.

Scattered thunderstorms erupted over the west Texas Trans Pecos region by midday on the 31st. The activity initiated over Pecos County where dangerous flash flooding conditions affected Fort Stockton. Convection spread northeast during the afternoon and evening into the Permian Basin. Officials reported that flash flooding "effectively shut down" the city of Andrews during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms and locally heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding for the second consecutive day over northeastern Crane County on the 31st. Local officials reported flood waters flowing over U.S. Highway 385 north of Crane.
The Texas Tech West Texas Mesonet station reported a 69 mph thunderstorm wind gust.
The NWS Cooperative Observer reported baseball size hail had broken windows.
Baseball size hail was reported by the FD.  The hail broke windows and damaged roofs.
Strong thunderstorm wind snapped power poles.
A large tree was blown down across Holly Cemetary Road near Highway 79 in east Franklin.
Highway 36, FM 1476, and Highway 16 in and around Comanche were impassable due to high water in low lying areas.  Some homes were also flooded.
Low water crossings in and around Hamilton were flooded.  Some roads in the area had to be barricaded.

Heavy rainfall farther north over eastern Pecos County resulted in a crest of twenty feet along the Pecos River just east of Sheffield around 06:00 CST. This crest combined with the continuation of heavy rainfall caused extensive flash flooding in rural areas east and southeast of the city. Shortly before 16:00 CST travelers on Interstate 10 witnessed a pickup truck and a camper trailer floating beneath the Pecos River bridge. A search downstream by local authorities ensued, but many rural location across along the river were inaccessible due to extensive flooding. It was later determined that the pickup and camper trailer were swept into the Pecos River by flash flooding on the Pecos River Ranch near Sheffield. The vehicle and trailer were vacant when they were overtaken by raging flood waters, and were ultimately found many miles down river near the Rio Grande. Several head of cattle also were killed in the flood waters.
Funnel spotted by a pilot west of David Wayne Hooks Airport.
